items.q: Why do computers use binary rather than denary?
items.correct: Hardware can reliably distinguish two states, such as high and low voltage, so two digits give dependable storage and processing.
items.misconception: Binary is used because it is quicker for humans to read.
items.random_wrong: Binary numbers are shorter than the same values in denary.
items.why: Students look for a human convenience reason. The choice is physical: two well-separated electrical states resist noise, where ten levels would be error-prone.

items.q: When overflow happens, does the computer just get the right answer with extra digits?
items.correct: No - the extra carry bit does not fit in the available storage, so the stored result is wrong unless the overflow is detected and handled.
items.misconception: Yes - the computer automatically grows the storage to fit any result.
items.random_wrong: Yes - overflow only changes the sign of the answer to positive.
items.why: On paper you just write another digit; in fixed-width registers there is nowhere for it to go. That mismatch between paper and hardware is the misconception.

items.q: Is hexadecimal a different kind of data from binary?
items.correct: No - hex is just a more compact way of writing the same values; every hex digit corresponds exactly to four binary bits.
items.misconception: Yes - computers store hex numbers separately from binary numbers.
items.random_wrong: Yes - hexadecimal can only represent colours.
items.why: Different symbols suggest different data. Base is only notation: 2F, 00101111 and 47 are the same stored value written three ways.

items.q: Hexadecimal is often used by programmers because it:
items.correct: Is shorter and easier for humans to read than long binary numbers
items.misconception: Is the only base computers can process
items.why: It's Is shorter and easier for humans to read than long binary numbers. “Is the only base computers can process” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: A calculation's result needs more bits than are available to store it
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A calculation's result needs more bits than are available to store it. “A file is saved twice” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A character set mapping characters to binary codes
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A character set mapping characters to binary codes. “A type of sound file” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Increasing the sample rate of a sound recording will:
items.correct: Improve the audio quality and increase the file size
items.misconception: Reduce the file size and the quality
items.random_wrong: Have no effect on the file at all
items.why: Correct answer: Improve the audio quality and increase the file size. “Reduce the file size and the quality” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the binary number 1100 in denary?
items.why: The real figure is 12. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
